message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We retrospectively studied US emergency department visits (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  =295,406), including nationally representative results. Patients were less likely to have detectable fevers during mornings, with especially large morning-evening differences during influenza outbreaks (national RR=0.56, 95%CI=0.47-0.66). This suggests morning screenings could miss otherwise-detectable cases. Twice-daily screenings could be a simple solution. However, similar COVID-19 research is needed.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Article Summary Line</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Fevers were about half as common in the morning as in the evening during influenza outbreaks, suggesting that mornings may be a bad time to perform once-daily fever screenings for infectious diseases, and that twice-daily screenings could be preferable.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Fevers Are Rarer in the Morning—Could We Be Missing Infectious Disease Cases by Screening for Fever Then?

reference.article-title: Fever incidence is much lower in the morning than the evening: Boston and US national triage data
